topic: How to create a Gmail account from scratch

inputs.topic: How to create a Gmail account from scratch

lesson.teacherObjective: By the end of this lesson, students can create a new Gmail account and explain each main screen they use.
teacherPrep: Check that the word Gmail is on the board.
Write one safe example email name on paper.
Put students in pairs for phone help.

teacherBoardLayout.title: Create a Gmail Account
rightColumn: Open Gmail and tap Create account
teacherBoardLayout.bottomLine: What screen are you on now?

teacherScript.say: Today we learn how to create a Gmail account. Email is a formal tool for school, work, and business. You will use your real name, not a nickname. A clean email name helps people trust you.
teacherScript.do: Write the title on the board. Ask students who has seen an email before. Point to the board points and tell them to copy them.

teacherScript.say: Think about Inkify in our area. Samke Jaca and Ntokozo Gwacela use email for quotes and invoices. WhatsApp helps with fast replies, but email helps keep a formal record. That is why students need a proper Gmail account. First, the phone opens the app. Next, the student chooses Create account. Then they type their real name, choose a username, and set a password. Some screens ask for a phone number, birthday, or gender. Students follow each screen one by one until Gmail says the account is ready.
teacherScript.do: Use short questions after each point. Ask students to name the next screen. Circle the words username and password on the board. Remind them that a username is the email name people see.

teacherScript.say: Now students begin the task on their phones or shared devices. One student holds the phone. One student reads the next step. One student checks the screen. Then they swap roles. If a step is not clear, the reader asks the teacher. Students must move through each screen in order. They must not rush ahead. They must stop when the account is created and they see the finish screen.
teacherScript.do: Walk between groups. Check that students tap the correct buttons and type their real names. Help anyone who gets stuck on username or password. Ask nearby groups to show you the screen they reached.

teacherScript.say: Let us check what you learned. What is the first thing you tap to start? Why do we use your real name? What is one screen that may ask for more details? If you can answer these, you understand the process.
teacherScript.do: Look for students who can say the first step in order. Listen for correct use of username and password. Ask two or three students to explain the next screen aloud.

lesson.teacherExplanation:
Today we are learning how to create a Gmail account. Email is a formal way to send messages to schools, businesses, and offices. It is also useful for job applications. A clean email name with your real name looks serious and helps people trust you.

Think about Inkify in our area. Samke Jaca and Ntokozo Gwacela use email when they send quotes and invoices to customers. WhatsApp is still useful for quick messages, but email gives a written record. In this lesson, students will follow each screen on their phone. They will open Gmail, tap Create account, type their name, choose a username, set a password, and finish the setup screens until the account is ready.

teacherVocabulary.simpleMeaning: a message sent through the internet
a secret word you use to enter an account
a personal login space on an app
formal and suitable for work or school

teacherDiscussionQuestions: Why is it better to use your real name in an email address?
How does Inkify use email differently from WhatsApp?
What might happen if two people try the same username?
lesson.teacherLocalExample: Inkify, run by Samke Jaca and Ntokozo Gwacela, uses email to send quotes and invoices to customers. They still use WhatsApp for quick replies, but email helps them keep a formal record. This shows why a Gmail account is useful for school, work, and business.

teacherDevicePlan.ifEnoughDevices: Let each student work on their own phone or PC. Ask them to pause at each new screen and raise a hand before moving on.
teacherDevicePlan.ifSharedDevices: Put 4–6 students in one group. One student taps, one reads, one watches for mistakes, and one writes the username and password safely. Rotate roles at each main screen.
teacherDevicePlan.ifNoInternet: Use the board and notebooks. Draw the Gmail flow from top to bottom: open app, create account, type name, choose username, set password, finish setup. Students copy the flow and write a sample email address using their real name.

teacherCommonMistakes.mistake: A student uses a nickname or funny email name.
teacherCommonMistakes.teacherResponse: Say: Use your real name. This is for school, jobs, and business, so it must look serious.

teacherCommonMistakes.mistake: A student gets stuck when the username is already taken.
teacherCommonMistakes.teacherResponse: Say: Choose a different username. Add your surname or a small number, then try again.
Say: Write it in a safe place. A password must stay secret, but you must remember it.

teacherAssessment: A student can name the first three Gmail setup steps in order.
A student can explain why a real name is better than a nickname.
A student can show the username they chose or write it in a notebook.
teacherTimeGuide: 15 min: student task or no-internet fallback
lesson.teacherWrapUpQuestion: What is one reason a Gmail account is useful for you?
lesson.teacherExtension: Faster groups can help a partner choose a better username and explain what makes it look professional.
lesson.taskIntro: You will have a new Gmail account that is ready to use for school, jobs, or customer messages.
lesson.taskReflection: You now have a new Gmail address. Write one real situation in the next few months where you will use it, who you will send the email to, and what the subject line will say.

steps.teach: Find the Gmail app in the Google Play Store. On the Gmail app page, you install the app if it is not already on your phone. If you already see Open, Gmail is already installed and you can still answer using what you see.
steps.question: After Gmail finishes downloading, what does the button change to?
steps.instruction: Open the Play Store, search for Gmail, open the Gmail app page, and tap Install if the button is there.
steps.visibleResult: You should see the download finish and the button change to Open.
steps.feedbackCorrect: Good — after Install finishes, the Gmail app page shows Open.
steps.feedbackWrong: Look at the button on the Gmail app page after the download finishes or if Gmail is already installed.
steps.tip: If you see Open at the start, Gmail is already on your phone.

steps.teach: Now you launch Gmail from the app page. This takes you into the app so you can start making your new account. If your phone looks a little different, look for the Open button on the Gmail page.
steps.question: Which button opens Gmail from the Play Store app page?
steps.feedbackCorrect: Correct — Open launches Gmail from the app page.
steps.feedbackWrong: Look on the Gmail app page for the button that appears after the app is installed.

steps.teach: The first Gmail screen gives you two choices. To make a brand new Gmail account, tap Create account, not Sign in. Sign in is only for people who already have an email account.
steps.question: Which button starts a brand new Gmail account?
steps.instruction: On the Welcome to Gmail screen, tap Create account.
steps.visibleResult: You should move to the next screen where Gmail asks what kind of account you want.
steps.feedbackCorrect: Yes — Create account starts the new Gmail signup.
steps.feedbackWrong: Go back to the Welcome to Gmail screen and look for the lower button that starts a new account.
steps.tip: Do not tap Sign in unless you already have a Gmail address.

steps.teach: Gmail now asks what type of account you want to create. Most students should choose For myself. This makes a normal personal Gmail account you can still use for school, jobs, and business messages.
steps.question: Which option should you tap to make your own Gmail account?
steps.feedbackCorrect: Right — For myself is the correct choice for your own Gmail account.
steps.feedbackWrong: Look at the list of account types and choose the one that means the account is for you.

steps.teach: On this screen, type your real first name and last name. A clean, real name looks more professional than a nickname. After you fill both boxes, tap Next.
steps.question: Type the name of the button you tap after entering your first name and last name.
steps.feedbackCorrect: Correct — Next takes you from the name screen to the username screen.
steps.feedbackWrong: Look below the name fields for the button that moves you forward.
steps.tip: Use your real name, not a nickname like 'coolboy' or 'babygirl'.

steps.teach: Now you choose your Gmail address in the Username field. Try a simple version of your real name. If Gmail says the name is taken, choose one of the suggestions or change it and then tap Next.
steps.question: Arrange these actions in the correct order for choosing your Gmail address.
tiles: Change it if the name is taken
correctOrder: Change it if the name is taken
steps.feedbackCorrect: Good — that is the right order for choosing your Gmail address.
steps.feedbackWrong: Start by typing a username before you look at suggestions or move to the next screen.
steps.tip: A simple address like your name is better than a joke name.

steps.teach: This screen asks for a password and the same password again in Confirm. Make it strong so other people cannot open your account. Use at least eight characters and include letters and numbers before you tap Next.
steps.question: Match each password idea to its meaning.

pairs.match: The secret word that protects your account
A password with enough characters and a number
The button that moves you to the next signup screen

steps.feedbackCorrect: Well done — you matched the password terms correctly.
steps.feedbackWrong: Look again at the words Password and Confirm and think about what each box is for.
steps.tip: Write your password in a safe place so you do not forget it.

steps.teach: Some phones show a screen for your mobile number and SMS code. Enter your number if asked, tap Next, then type the code you receive. This helps protect your account if you lose your password later.
steps.question: What do you type after Gmail sends a message to your phone?
steps.instruction: If your phone shows a phone number screen, enter your number, tap Next, and type the SMS code you receive. If this screen does not appear, continue to the next signup screen.
steps.visibleResult: You should either move past the phone check or see the signup continue after the SMS code is accepted.
steps.feedbackCorrect: Correct — you type the SMS code to verify your phone number.
steps.feedbackWrong: Look at the message Google sends to your phone and enter the numbers from that message.
steps.tip: If this screen does not appear, Gmail may let you continue without phone verification.

steps.teach: You may see a screen asking for a recovery email. This is optional on many phones. If you have another email, you can add it. If not, skip it if the app allows and then tap Next.
steps.question: If you do not have another email address, what can you usually do on the recovery email screen?
steps.feedbackCorrect: Yes — you can usually skip the recovery email step and continue.
steps.feedbackWrong: Look at what this screen is asking for and think about what happens if you do not have another email.
steps.tip: Only add a recovery email if it is really yours and you can open it later.

steps.teach: Now Gmail asks for your birth date. Select your month, day, and year carefully, then tap Next. Use your real details so your account information is correct.
steps.question: Which details do you enter on this screen before tapping Next?
steps.feedbackCorrect: Right — this screen is for your birth date.
steps.feedbackWrong: Look at the fields on this screen and choose the personal detail Google is asking for here.
steps.tip: Check the year carefully before you continue.

steps.teach: After your birth date, Gmail may ask you to choose your gender. Select the option that fits you, then tap Next. If your phone layout looks different, the Next button still moves you forward.
steps.question: Type the name of the button you tap after choosing your gender.
steps.feedbackCorrect: Correct — Next takes you from the gender screen to Privacy and Terms.
steps.feedbackWrong: Look below the gender options for the button that continues the signup.

steps.teach: This screen explains Google's Privacy and Terms. You may need to scroll first. When you are ready, tap I Agree to accept and finish setting up your Gmail account.
steps.question: Which button accepts the Privacy and Terms and lets you continue?
steps.instruction: Scroll through the Privacy and Terms screen if needed, then tap I Agree.
steps.visibleResult: You should move to the final account setup screen or straight into Gmail.
steps.feedbackCorrect: Good — I Agree accepts the Privacy and Terms and completes the signup.
steps.feedbackWrong: Look at the bottom of the Privacy and Terms screen for the button that accepts the agreement.
steps.tip: If the button is hidden, scroll down first.

steps.teach: Your new Gmail account is now ready. When setup is complete, Gmail opens your inbox. Seeing the inbox means the account was created successfully and you can start using email like Inkify does for clear, professional messages.
steps.question: What screen shows that your Gmail account is ready to use?
steps.instruction: Finish the last setup screen and look for the Gmail inbox.
steps.visibleResult: You should see your Gmail inbox with the Compose button visible.
steps.feedbackCorrect: Excellent — the Gmail inbox shows your new account is ready to use.
steps.feedbackWrong: Look for the main Gmail screen with your messages list and the Compose button.
steps.tip: If you do not see the inbox yet, wait a few seconds for Gmail to finish loading.

lesson.teacherPlanHTML:
Chalk-and-board lesson plan

Create a Gmail Account

By the end of this lesson, students can create a new Gmail account and explain each main screen they use.

Student task: Create a Gmail Account

1Before Class

  • Check that the word Gmail is on the board.
  • Write one safe example email name on paper.
  • Put students in pairs for phone help.
Blackboard Plan
Use this instead of a projector. Students copy the key words and steps.
Create a Gmail Account
Key words
  • username: your email name
  • password: secret login word
Class steps
  • Open Gmail and tap Create account
  • Type your name and username
  • Finish the setup screens
What screen are you on now?

2Teach It Directly

No computer or big screen needed. Read, adapt, and use the board.
Open 5 min
Say: Today we learn how to create a Gmail account. Email is a formal tool for school, work, and business. You will use your real name, not a nickname. A clean email name helps people trust you.
Do: Write the title on the board. Ask students who has seen an email before. Point to the board points and tell them to copy them.
Explain 10 min
Say: Think about Inkify in our area. Samke Jaca and Ntokozo Gwacela use email for quotes and invoices. WhatsApp helps with fast replies, but email helps keep a formal record. That is why students need a proper Gmail account. First, the phone opens the app. Next, the student chooses Create account. Then they type their real name, choose a username, and set a password. Some screens ask for a phone number, birthday, or gender. Students follow each screen one by one until Gmail says the account is ready.
Do: Use short questions after each point. Ask students to name the next screen. Circle the words username and password on the board. Remind them that a username is the email name people see.
Practice 15 min
Say: Now students begin the task on their phones or shared devices. One student holds the phone. One student reads the next step. One student checks the screen. Then they swap roles. If a step is not clear, the reader asks the teacher. Students must move through each screen in order. They must not rush ahead. They must stop when the account is created and they see the finish screen.
Do: Walk between groups. Check that students tap the correct buttons and type their real names. Help anyone who gets stuck on username or password. Ask nearby groups to show you the screen they reached.
Check 5 min
Say: Let us check what you learned. What is the first thing you tap to start? Why do we use your real name? What is one screen that may ask for more details? If you can answer these, you understand the process.
Do: Look for students who can say the first step in order. Listen for correct use of username and password. Ask two or three students to explain the next screen aloud.

3Key Words

email
a message sent through the internet
username
the name before @gmail.com
password
a secret word you use to enter an account
account
a personal login space on an app
i-akhawunti
professional
formal and suitable for work or school
umuntu oyingcweti

4Local Example

Inkify, run by Samke Jaca and Ntokozo Gwacela, uses email to send quotes and invoices to customers. They still use WhatsApp for quick replies, but email helps them keep a formal record. This shows why a Gmail account is useful for school, work, and business.

5Discussion Questions

  1. 1Why is it better to use your real name in an email address?
  2. 2How does Inkify use email differently from WhatsApp?
  3. 3What might happen if two people try the same username?

6Device And No-Internet Plan

Enough devicesLet each student work on their own phone or PC. Ask them to pause at each new screen and raise a hand before moving on.
Shared devicesPut 4–6 students in one group. One student taps, one reads, one watches for mistakes, and one writes the username and password safely. Rotate roles at each main screen.
No internetUse the board and notebooks. Draw the Gmail flow from top to bottom: open app, create account, type name, choose username, set password, finish setup. Students copy the flow and write a sample email address using their real name.

7Common Mistakes

Student may...
A student uses a nickname or funny email name.
Teacher response
Say: Use your real name. This is for school, jobs, and business, so it must look serious.
Student may...
A student gets stuck when the username is already taken.
Teacher response
Say: Choose a different username. Add your surname or a small number, then try again.
Student may...
A student forgets the password.
Teacher response
Say: Write it in a safe place. A password must stay secret, but you must remember it.

8Check Understanding

  • A student can name the first three Gmail setup steps in order.
  • A student can explain why a real name is better than a nickname.
  • A student can show the username they chose or write it in a notebook.

9Wrap Up

What is one reason a Gmail account is useful for you?

10Fast Groups

Faster groups can help a partner choose a better username and explain what makes it look professional.
Time Guide — 30 minutes
5 minopening and board
10 minexplanation
15 minstudent task or no-internet fallback
5 mincheck and wrap-up
